There is indeed a lack of documentation for this at the moment. This is definitely a supported feature. In order to use pretrained embeddings you need to call `python -m sockeye.init_embedding`.

Quoting from the CLI documentation:
```
Initializing Sockeye embedding weights with pretrained word representations.
It also supports updating vocabulary-sized weights for a new vocabulary.
Quick usage:
    python3 -m sockeye.init_embedding        \
            -w embed-in-src.npy embed-in-tgt.npy    \
            -i vocab-in-src.json vocab-in-tgt.json   \
            -o vocab-out-src.json vocab-out-tgt.json  \
            -n source_embed_weight target_embed_weight \
            -f params.init
Optional arguments:
    --weight-files, -w
        list of input weight files in .npy, .npz or Sockeye parameter format
        .npy: a single array with shape=(vocab-in-size, embedding-size/hidden-size)
        .npz: a dictionary of {parameter_name: array}
              parameter_name is given by "--names"
        Sockeye parameter: the parameter name is given by "--names"
    --vocabularies-in, -i
        list of input vocabularies as token-index dictionaries in .json format
    --vocabularies-out, -o
        list of output vocabularies as token-index dictionaries in .json format
        They can be generated using sockeye.vocab before actual Sockeye training.
    --names, -n
        list of Sockeye parameter names for embedding weights (or other vocabulary-sized weights)
        Most common ones are source_embed_weight, target_embed_weight, source_target_embed_weight,
        target_output_weight and target_output_bias.
    Sizes of above 4 lists should be exactly the same - they are vertically aligned.
    --file, -f
        file to write initialized parameters
    --encoding, -c
        open input vocabularies with specified encoding (default: utf-8)
```

This gives you a file `params.init`, which you then in turn can use in `sockeye.train` through the `--params` parameter. I hope this helps.

I'm thinking that `BookmarkParent` should be subclassed by `docx.blkcntr.BlockItemContainer`, rather than `Body` itself (`Body` subclasses `BlockItemContainer`).

`BlockItemContainer` already has an `._element` attribute (which `Body` inherits, by the way), so no need for the `if hasattr()` bit. In general, any element "can" have an `._element` attribute, so if we found a proxy object that didn't have that, we would just add it in the constructor, e.g. `self._element = self._body = body`. I realized part-way through that it would be important to have a more abstract name for the proxied element, which is when I started using `._element` instead of or in addition to for example `._body`. I just didn't go back and add it everywhere it was missing; I just add it in as I encounter a need for it.

You can differentiate `OK` from `ZERO_RESULTS` by inspecting the length of the result, zero returned objects is `ZERO_RESULTS`. The reason we inspect is that both `OK` and `ZERO_RESULTS` are successful results, while everything else are failure conditions that we need to throw error conditions on.

Hi @domesticmouse, you can actually display directions without the geometry library. In this example: http://jsfiddle.net/user2314737/u9no8te4/ you can see that a DirectionsRenderer object (represented by the variable directionsDisplay) can take as input the full response from a directions call. So in this specific case, if you don't provide the complete data structure, it doesn't work - even if you're just omitting "status" or other optional parts like "geocoded_waypoints". I asked the question because if the library returned the full response, then it could be used in this scenario, and I was a bit confused about this. But as I said, I managed to do what I wanted following a very similar approach to what @rbouchez suggested, and I'm happy with the library.

@jld23 Thank you for asking me to outline tests.  By doing that I was able to think through this and make it more concise.

I changed the %%SAS cell magic into a line_cell_magic.  The cell magic version, %%SAS, performs as it did previously.  The line magic version, %SAS, lets a user submit SAS code as a line magic.  This can be handy for a quick libname statement or %LET statement for instance. 

The %SASLOG line magic allows the user to recall the log for the most recent submission.  With a "-FULL" option the user can request the full SAS log for the current session.

The %%SASPROC cell magic was added and also works as a %SASPROC line magic.  This simplifies the submission of any SAS proc including interactive and non-interactive procs.

I tested the functionality and attempted to document the use of these magics in a Jupyter notebook named: Test Magics SAS SASPROC adn SASLOG.ipynb

If you want to achieve a syntax close to what you showed above; we'll have to change the save method on the model to return a model object rather than result.

```python
    def save(self, **kwargs):
        payload = self.__dict__
        if "id" not in self.__dict__:
            logger.info("creating a {}".format(self.service))
            result = self.create(payload, **kwargs)
        else:
            result = self.modify(payload, id=self.id, **kwargs)
        return type(self)(result)
```

returning a `type(self)(result)` can achieve that. Then we can modify the Report Model to something like this

```python
class Report(Model):

    def download(self, **kwargs):
        return self.client.get("report-download", id=self.report_id)
```

with which you'll be able to do 

```
# Create a report
report = Report(json).save()

# Download the report
report.download()
```

@petergerten I'm not 100% sure on the docker-py call (as I use `docker-compose` which uses `docker-py` to do this) but I believe when you call `create_container`, you need to specify `runtime="nvidia"`

The new nvidia-docker2 adds a runtime to docker daemon, so using the nvidia-docker2 plugin is as simple as specifying the nvidia runtime. For example, using the docker-cli:

```
docker run -it --rm nvidia/cuda nvidia-smi
```

**Fails**

But

```
docker run --runtime=nvidia -it --rm nvidia/cuda nvidia-smi
```

**Succeeds!**

If this docker-cli test doesn't work for you, then I believe you don't have nvidia-docker2 installed successfully.

---

## Background

The `nvidia-docker` cli used to be a shim that sets up the nvidia specific settings for you (volumes, devices, etc...) if and only if a [label existed](https://gitlab.com/nvidia/cuda/blob/f31ece4bc9195a446f1dd6b9c8479c71fb295dfa/8.0/runtime/Dockerfile#L30). This is what this PR was originally reproducing. Now the `nvidia-docker2` cli is a script that just adds the `--runtime=nvidia` flag for you. This new runtime is a modified version of the docker default runtime, that will add the nvidia specific volumes, devices, etc if and only if an [environment variable](https://gitlab.com/nvidia/cuda/blob/f31ece4bc9195a446f1dd6b9c8479c71fb295dfa/8.0/runtime/Dockerfile#L40-42) is set.

So because `runtime` is part of the `docker`, `docker-py`, and `docker-compose` API, there is no need for this PR.

Also bear in mind that

$ docker logs -f <container>
```

is not an equivalent of

```

```

I like the idea of having self-contained UFOs for MTI-based projects. However one can't just drop arbitrary files in the top-level UFO directory. Changing the UFO spec is doable, but would require some time.
The format has a `data` folder exactly for the purpose of storing private, application specific data.

http://unifiedfontobject.org/versions/ufo3/data/

We could have fontmake place the MTI source file in there, and then ufo2ft would read from there and call mtiLib to compile the master ttfs. As the reverse domain name for the data subfolder, we could use `com.github.googlei18n.ufo2ft.mtiFeatures` or something similar.

Technically, one may also store arbitrary `userData` inside the .glyphs format itself, and glyphsLib could write that out to the appropriate UFO data folder when converting to UFO. That way, the .glyphs file itself could be made self-contained.

Hi,
I have some good news for everyone.  In my research I came across a project that was mentioned on a couple of gevent+asyncio threads which was supposed to make the two libs compatible, but the repo and pypi package had been... erased from existence.  Luckily, someone made [a clone of it](https://github.com/2mf/aiogevent) before it was deleted.  It looked promising so I tracked down the original author (none other than the author of trollius, Victor Stinner) to ask him if there was a particular reason that he abandoned the project.  He said, paraphrasing, "no, just lost interest". 

Using it is as simple as:

```python
asyncio.set_event_loop_policy(aiogevent.EventLoopPolicy())
```

Seemed too good to be true, so I tried it out.  I ran the asyncio tests from txaio (from master branch) and autobahn, patched to run using the aiogevent gevent-based event loop, and they all passed.  This is a reasonable demonstration that the eventloop works, but it doesn't prove that asyncio and gevent can peacefully coexist.  For that I've been working on a separate set of tests: gist [here](https://gist.github.com/chadrik/a7b7d8abce891978a7dbfd0d086828b3).  Long story short, as long as your gevent-based code runs below a greenlet that has been specially prepared and handed to the ascyncio eventloop it should run cooperatively with asyncio. 

Assuming I don't run into any gotchas, it would appear that this allows gevent to play nice with autobahn without any work on your part!

There are at least 3 issues on the gevent project regarding asyncio, so I'm going to bring my findings there as well, to see if they can help me poke holes in it.

Let me know if you have any questions, but I'm reasonably confident that this is the way forward, so we can close this issue.

Well, currently the function is just ``return maybeDeferred(func, *args, **kw)`` ;) and txaio tries really, really hard not to do any "wrapping" etc (as there are typically a *lot* of Deferreds/Futures produced). Also, this has kind of "magical" downstream effects on users-of-users of ``txaio`` -- even if that case is super rare.

What about a separate API for this? Perhaps it's even possible to implement it as a helper-style function. Since the actual use-case is to issue a nicer error when an Autobahn ApplicationSession subclass implements ``onJoin`` (etc) incorrectly, it's really up to the user of txaio to decide when they want these semantics should apply.

So, e.g., ``txaio.error_if_generator(instance.some_method)`` or something would raise when:

 * using twisted and ``some_method`` is a generator-function but not ``@inlineCallbacks``-decorated
 * using asyncio and ``some_method`` is a generator-function but not ``@coroutine``-decorated

Then, this could be used in e.g. Autobahn to detect if user-methods are "just" bare generators. Thus, Autobahn could choose to either provide a "way out" (e.g. decorator) or simply declare "you cannot use a method that produces a generator for ``onJoin``, etc."

I'm happy to see an early prototype, but I hope this doesn't blunt our efforts to reach consensus for the formal WAMP-SCRAM specification. Having said that, whatever practical issues you're come up against while implementing this early prototype would certainly be worth learning about.

@meejah 
> - wamp-scram-argon2id19
> - wamp-scram-argon2id19-plus
> - wamp-scram-pbkdf2
> - wamp-scram-pbkdf2-plus

The "-plus" suffix thing was a carry-over from the SCRAM RFC, and I don't think it's really needed for WAMP-SCRAM. Channel binding is something that has to be requested by the client, and there's already a `gs2_cbind_flag` property that indicates such a request (with `gs2_cbind_flag` to be possibly renamed to `channel_binding`.

Also, FYI, we're leaning towards "SRP" over "SCRAM" for our project because it's more secure against evesdropping. I was going to write an SRP proposal soon. A lot of the things we're working out for the WAMP-SCRAM spec can be used for a WAMP-SRC spec.

To make this more general, how about adding an optional `post_opcode_callback` (or `pre_opcode_callback`) parameter at the lowest level? The callback could inspect the state of the computation and, if necessary, raise an exception (or return `False`) to abort the execution. Miners could add some `check_gas_payment` as a callback, but this could also be used for other things (mainly debugging and testing, but maybe also as an easy to access but low level entry point for chain analysis or contract watcher tools). Disadvantage would be that the parameter would have to be added to the whole stack of `apply_computation` functions and friends, so it's not as non-invasive as the mixin solution.

This is the important part of that message:
> ` Method Not Allowed for url: https://rinkeby.infura.io/`

Infura does not support remote addresses. On mainnet, do not send your private keys or passwords to any remote service! If you do accidentally, you should empty the account and never use it again, and change the password.

In Web3 v3, sending transactions to Infura is a bit cumbersome. (One needs to sign the transaction locally, and send with `w3.eth.sendRawTransaction`). Either use a local geth/parity/etc node, or upgrade to the beta v4, which has some convenience tools for signing transactions locally. There is an ongoing project to sign transactions locally using middleware that makes it feel like you're using accounts with private keys on the remote node, but it will probably be at least a couple weeks until it is released. #493

Because of channel priority, if your .condarc file contains

```
channels:
  - conda-forge
  - defaults
```

the non-mkl packages of `conda-forge` will be preferred over mkl-enabled packages in `defaults`.  To fix this just change your .condarc to 

```
channels:
  - defaults
  - conda-forge
```

The possibility I had in mind was someone switching the CPU for one with the same socket that supports new instruction set extensions such as SSE4.1 and getting the warning above. However, as I dig a little deeper it appears that instruction set extensions are exactly the same for all the CPUs that fit any given socket, even if marketing terms such as "Core i7" include CPUs with different sockets and different instruction set extensions. More generally, I agree that people changing a CPU, motherboard, or GPU will expect that they need to reinstall everything or at least take some extra steps to fall back to generic versions, so as you say, no need to worry about this.

Merged into #4474, which at this point won't be 4.3.x.  But I want to keep pushing forward on that PR for a little bit longer and see where we end up.  The direction I'm headed there is to only be making occasional use of the `Dist.parse_dist_name()` static method to maintain old interfaces where we have to.  There's still a lot of work to do though.

Hello, indeed conda and anaconda have no man page 

% zsh find anaconda/share/man/  -name "*conda*"

does not return anything.

However, to provide help, they have the option -h .

If any man pages were created, they should go into anaconda/share/man/man1 or anaconda/share/man/man3, etc.,

so they will be found by man, in so far as man path is properly configured.  

This is done in general by setting the environment variable $MANPATH.
However, for Mac OS X system wide installation, this can be done by adding a file in /etc/manpaths.d/.

Other anaconda unix executables have man pages.

% man --path xmllint
/Applications/anaconda/share/man/man1/xmllint.1

For Mac OS X single user no sudo installaton, you will need to touch $MANPATH and unfortunately this may break the /etc/manpaths system.  See % man path_helper.

Hey, wait. Can you look in the directory where you ran this, and confirm the existence of a file called '=4.1'?

Looks like this is an interaction with the shell; it is interpreting '>' as a redirection. Try this:
```
conda install "nodejs>=4.1"
```
What's happening is that it's redirecting conda's output to `=4.1`, and then it's waiting for you to respond for input---but because the output is gone, you don't know it's waiting for that :-)

Thanks for submitting this.  

First, you mention that you're trying to delete `metadata-curator-[timestamp]` and `snapshot-curator-[timestamp]` and it deletes these.  However, the name in the error is `curator-20150213001500`.  Was this correct?  If so, is that snapshot supposed to be deleted?

Regardless, I have racked my brain to try to guess how this scenario could happen.  One of three explanations I can guess at are as follows.

## Duplicate snapshots in the working list.

This theory falls apart because of https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/master/curator/cli/snapshot_selection.py#L81
Before any work is done, the list is turned into a set (only one of anything in a set), then back into a list again.  This basically guarantees a unique list.

## Individual nodes are not connected to the repository properly.

The error, `(Service: Amazon S3; Status Code: 404; Error Code: NoSuchKey; Request ID: 5531B70DBC6BD25B)`, indicates that the Elasticsearch S3 plugin can't find the file.

While this is concerning, it's not something Curator itself has any control over.  It makes a call via the Elasticsearch python module, and if that call cannot be completed due to a remote exception, there's really nothing Curator can do.  Curator can neither create this scenario, nor correct it.

I _can_ try to put some better error handling in to catch this sort of failure, but that will only be cosmetic to this scenario.

Ideas to troubleshoot/fix this include updating the S3 plugin and/or your Elasticsearch version.

## Snapshot is in a bad state

The snapshot in the error, `curator-20150213001500`, may be incomplete, or corrupted in some way.  It shows up in the snapshot metadata, but the file has been deleted.  This could also happen if it were incompletely deleted in a previous attempt (i.e. the files were deleted, but the metadata was not, so it appeared that the snapshot was still there), or the snapshot was malformed when created.

## Cluster issues

This may indicate something amiss with your cluster.  One node may be able to see something another cannot.  

## Conclusion

In any case, with this not caused by having the snapshot name in the list 2x, this is outside the scope of Curator.  It is possible I've missed something, but these are my best guesses as to how this state could have happened.

Currently there is no way to make an operation timeout inside elasticsearch. The `_msearch` API doesn't accept `timeout` parameter.

Most you can do is specify the timeout on the client, which is done either by globally setting the `timeout` parameter on the client itself (`Elasticsearch(timeout=12)`) in seconds or by passing in `request_timeout` during the API call.

See http://elasticsearch-py.readthedocs.org/en/master/api.html#timeout

Regarding the complexity I would like to justify why this change can be interesting.The following code shows a situation where 10 request to the same domain are made when there is a cache miss

```python
import asyncio
import aiohttp

from aiohttp.connector import TCPConnector
from aiohttp.resolver import DefaultResolver

class MyResolver(DefaultResolver):

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        self.cnt = 0
        super(MyResolver, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)

    async def resolve(self, *args, **kwargs):
        self.cnt += 1
        return (await super(MyResolver, self).resolve(*args, **kwargs))



async def fetch(session, url):
    await session.get(url)

async def main():
    url = 'http://www.vilaweb.cat/'
    resolver = MyResolver()
    connector = TCPConnector(resolver=resolver, use_dns_cache=True)
    session = aiohttp.ClientSession(connector=connector)
    tasks = [fetch(session, url) for i in range(10)]
    await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
    print('DNS Resolve calls %d' % resolver.cnt)
    await fetch(session, url)
    print('DNS Resolve calls %d' % resolver.cnt)
    await fetch(session, url)
    print('DNS Resolve calls %d' % resolver.cnt)
    await fetch(session, url)
    print('DNS Resolve calls %d' % resolver.cnt)
    await fetch(session, url)
    print('DNS Resolve calls %d' % resolver.cnt)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    asyncio.get_event_loop().run_until_complete(main())
```

This is _by design_. First consecutive calls will be paused at the same code path, and they wont be resumed until the next full scheduler iteration. This MR get rid of that issue having as a result only one DNS query.

We *do* get the mtime right after writing it! This check doesn't rely on what Jupyter thinks the time is.

I'm guessing that just after we write it, the filesystem has it in a local cache, so the mtime it gives us is based on the client's clock. Then at some point later, it gets confirmation that the server has stored the new data, and switches to an mtime based on a server timestamp. So the next time we get the mtime, it has changed, exactly as if something else had written the file in the meantime.

In theory one could use tags as an inverse from the current tag removal rules and only include those cells that are tagged. This would mean a new preprocessor, but that's not too much. 

@toqduj if you're interested, I'm going to be on vacation for a while, but you can look at https://github.com/jupyter/nbconvert/blob/master/nbconvert/preprocessors/tagremove.py to get an idea of what would need to be implemented. 

Then it would be a matter of tagging the cells you want and exporting using an appropriate config file.

If you want multiple individual cells exported as separate files, you could also make it more complicated and instead of doing it as a single pass operation, create a separate document for each of your tags that you specify as being included. If you wanted only individual cells for this model that would require using a unique tag for each… but that's not impossible. This would probably be implemented as a separate exporter and not included in nbconvert core (though I could be persuaded otherwise). I would use the model we have for handling external output files in the notebook by writing a zip. That would mean that it would work with the eventual nbconvert service that I'm working on (which communicates RESTfully so it can only return a single file).

There's also another approach (that works but isn't general enough for nbconvert AFAICT) where you use one tag, and each cell with that tag is exported to a separate document. This would be implemented at the exporter level.

Yeah, the docs have an incorrect depiction of the situation; the first argument is always the class.

This is because you can run validation on a plain dict without ever creating an instance. So, there might not be an instance to pass. (It *would* be possible to pass an instance when one exists, though, but this is not currently implemented.)

Essentially, the validators are implicit classmethods. It turns out that the discovery mechanism actually gets confused by a `classmethod` decorator. I've put together [a patch](https://github.com/schematics/schematics/compare/classmethod-validators). Even though the decorators are redundant, this allows them be used.

For someone not familiar with Schematics, it may be confusing to see `cls` as the first parameter when there's no `classmethod` around, so I suppose it makes sense that explicit decorators can be added for clarity.

Before I merge the patch, I'd like to ask for some opinions here:

* Does everybody agree with allowing the decorator, or is there a downside to having two different conventions?
* Would there be any point in passing an instance to the validator when one exists? If yes, should the decision be based on
    * whether the validator has been designated as a classmethod or
    * whether the first argument is `cls` or `self`?

What about when the validator wants `self` but we don't have an instance? It looks like this could get confusing very fast.

Don't agree with 1). I prefer validation to raise an exception, containing the error messages. I think storing the errors in the model it's more awkward and less flexible. I don't want an external library to mess around with my data structures. What's wrong with an exception?

Not really sure if 2) is needed as well, I mean, it's just trivial to implement that functionality yourself without bloating the API with extra flags.

Just IMO.

regions.  But it's also a handy tool to have when eyeballing calls on the

After a closer look, this is because the gzip implementation in dfvfs only supports gzip files with a single member (see http://www.zlib.org/rfc-gzip.html). _ReadFileFooter() just reads the last 8 bytes to determine the "file footer": https://github.com/log2timeline/dfvfs/blob/master/dfvfs/file_io/gzip_file_io.py#L133 but in reality, this is the trailer of the last compressed member in the file, not a true file footer.

I actually think this is a duplicate of #1882. Both address the problem of requesting a certificate whose names are a subset of those an existing certificate. With that said, I appreciate you taking the time to write up such a detailed post with both suggestions and examples.

The approach we're likely to take to solve #1882 mirrors how we handled the opposite situation. This is where you are requesting a certificate whose names are a superset of those in an existing certificate. In this case, assuming you provided nothing on the command line, you'll see the following:
```
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
You have an existing certificate that contains a portion of the domains you
requested (ref: /etc/letsencrypt/renewal/example.com.conf)

It contains these names: example.com, www.example.com

You requested these names for the new certificate: example.com,
www.example.com, test.example.com

Do you want to expand and replace this existing certificate with the new
certificate?
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
(E)xpand/(C)ancel: 
```

You can also skip this prompt by providing command line flags. If you provide `--expand`, on the command line, the symlinks for the existing certificate will be updated to point to the certificate with the new name.

If, however, you provide `--duplicate`, a new directory will be created to store your certificate. The name of this directory is determined by the first domain name that you entered. If there is another directory with that name, it will append a four digit number so that the name is unique. Otherwise, it uses the domain name with no suffix.

I believe this approach gets you all of the behavior you want with the exception of being able specify to specify the name of the new directory on the command line. If you think this would be useful, let me know and I'll create a new issue (or feel free to do it yourself). Alternatively, if you feel that this is a different problem than the one expressed in #1882, let me know and I'll reopen.

While I do see where you are coming from, I have to disagree with you. Having the client listen on ipv6 is relevant *right now* because of NAT64.

What is NAT64, you may ask?
Basically, the network admin can set aside a block of ipv6 (the RFC range is 64:ff9b::/96) which will be 1:1 mapped to the ipv4 internet (basically, if you try to connect to the nat64 encoded version of an ipv4 it will automatically route it through to the public ipv4 (and a reverse path exists in my case)).
I am in fact in posession of an ipv4 address, it is however not directly attached to the server in question (it's behind the NAT64). Any connections to my ipv4 arrive as ipv6 on my machine. I've been able to work around the bug by using the manual option and doing some shenanigans to the given python code to make it listen on ipv6. (I can't use my web server directly due to the machine in question functioning primarily as a load balancer, as such it does not have the capability to serve a directory using an ipv6 enabled server like nginx or apache.)

The issue is that plugin_arguments get added through a call to `add_plugin_args` which in turn pulls plugin parsers from `six` and calls `add_argument`
This presents a few problems.

1. The `modify_arg_for_default_detection` line is in HelpfulArgParser's `add` command, not in `add_argument`
2. The parsers used for this are `SilentParsers` not helpful
3. These parsers don't necessarily have detect_default set

it'd be nice to have SilentParser have the same implementation of HelpfulArgParses modify_arg_for_default_detection, and then we could add a detect_default flag to it before adding plugin args - that would solve this problem

queued.min.messages is still limited (or maybe unlimited) to the message set size returned by the broker (originally batched by the producer). What this means in practice is that even if you have queued.min.messages=10 and 200 byte messages, a fetched message set may contain thousands of such messages and all of them will be enqueued.
If your per-message processing is long even a small queue like that may stall stats callbacks, et.al.

I just opened #219, which does seem to fix my problematic case. However, I have some misgivings about the whole approach. We are effectively ordering things by the `mod_time` of each version, but the `mod_time` does not necessarily reflect the order of the updates (which _is_ reflected in the iteration order of the versions).

So what I'd really like `keepDays` to do is to preserve whatever I uploaded `N` days ago. But because it's trusting these possibly out-of-order `mod_time`s, you can get weird cases. For example, imagine you had these versions (which were posted in this order):

```
(1) id=A, mod_time=100
(2) <hidden>, mod_time=300
(3) id=B, mod_time=200
```

and you set `keepDays` to cut off at `time=400`. Strictly following the chain in order, the most recent thing we saw was `B`. But we won't keep that. We'll bump the hide-marker to the "front" of the chain of deletions, drop everything else, and then drop the hide-marker as worthless (since it has nothing behind it).

But would the user be happier if we _kept_ `B`? Clearly something funny is happening with the `mod_time` on the original client that pushed these up (out of order clocks, or perhaps step 3 was correcting an old mistake by restoring the file from a version with an older `mtime`).

So I actually wonder if @bwbeach's original is doing the right thing: walk backwards and find the most recent version (by chain order!) that needs deleting, and skip deleting that to keep it as the sentinel value. Before I work on a pull request, I wanted to get approval for my solution. Feel free to correct me if I am getting anything wrong, since this will be my first real bug fix in open source:
I studied the code and this here is the function to be replaced in Python 3: 
in `marshmellow.utils`:
```python
def get_func_args2(func):
    """Given a callable, return a tuple of argument names. Handles
    `functools.partial` objects and class-based callables.
    """
    if isinstance(func, functools.partial):
        return inspect.getargspec(func.func).args
    if inspect.isfunction(func) or inspect.ismethod(func):
        return inspect.getargspec(func).args
    # Callable class
    return inspect.getargspec(func.__call__).args
```

It is called only once in `marshmellow.fields`:
```python
def _call_or_raise(self, func, value, attr):
        if len(get_func_args(func)) > 1:
            if self.parent.context is None:
                msg = 'No context available for Function field {0!r}'.format(attr)
                raise ValidationError(msg)
            return func(value, self.parent.context)
        else:
            return func(value)
```
###The problem
In Python 3 `inspect.getargspec(func).args` is replaced by `inspect.signature(func).parameters`. They work differently and signature is kind of smarter as it also can deal with partials.

###Proposed solution
Because of these differences in the two functions, I would build a new `get_func_arg` for Python 3 and put it into marshmellow.utils and then I would have marshmellow.compat make the switch which function to use. 
My new function looks like this: 
```python
def get_func_args3(func):
    """Given a callable, return a tuple of argument names. Handles
    `functools.partial` objects and class-based callables.
    """
    if inspect.isfunction(func) or inspect.ismethod(func) or isinstance(func, functools.partial):
        return inspect.signature(func).parameters
    elif inspect.isclass(func) and callable(func):
        return inspect.signature(func.__call__).parameters
```
I did not like the else branch in the old function and rather made that more specific, would that be okay? Or should there still be an else branch, that raises an error?

in marshmellow.compat I would add this branching:
```python
    ... 
    from marshmallow.utils import get_func_args2 as get_func_args
else:
    ... 
    from marshmallow.utils import get_func_args3 as get_func_args
```

###Approval or Feedback
What do you think: shall I go ahead with this, or can you please give me some feedback and tell me some other way to solve this?

```python
"""
Bilinear interpolation for Linke turbidity factor.
"""

import scipy.io as scio
import numpy as np
import os, sys
from pvlib.clearsky import lookup_linke_turbidity
import pandas as pd
from functools import partial
import logging

logging.basicConfig()
LOGGER = logging.getLogger(__name__)
LOGGER.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)

LTSHAPE = (2160, 4320)  # get this from mat file?
DX, DY = 180.0 / LTSHAPE[0], 360.0 / LTSHAPE[1]
LTFILE = os.path.join('data', 'LinkeTurbidities.mat')


class LinkeConfig:
    ltfile = LTFILE
    data = scio.loadmat(ltfile)
    linke_turbidity_table = data['LinkeTurbidity'] / 20.0


def wikipedia_interp2d(x, y, f, ix, iy, dx=DX, dy=DY):
    """
    Bilinear interpolation for (x, y) bounded in the rectangle between nodes
    (x1, y1) and (x1 + dx, y1 + dx).

    :param x: desired x coordinate
    :param y: desired y coordinate
    :param f: the output field
    :param ix: lower bounding nodes in x-direction
    :param iy: lower bounding nodes in y-direction
    :param dx: spacing between x nodes, default = 1
    :param dy: spacing between y nodes, default = 1
    https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bilinear_interpolation
    """
    x1, y1 = float(ix), float(iy)
    x2, y2 = x1 + dx, y1 + dy  # upper bounding nodes
    return np.dot(np.dot([x2 - x, x - x1], f).T, [[y2 - y], [y - y1]]) / dx / dy


def get_linke_turbidity_at_latlon(latitude, longitude):
    """
    Get the Linke Turbidity from the ``LinkeTurbidities.mat`` data file.
    :param latitude: latitude [degrees]
    :type latitude: float
    :param longitude: longitude
    :type longitude: float
    :return: Linke turbdity factor
    :rtype: np.array
    """
    lat, lon = latitude, longitude
    # int() always rounds towards zero
    ilat_0 = int((90.0 - lat) / DX)  # lower bound
    # avoid out of bounds indices
    if ilat_0 < 0 or ilat_0 > LTSHAPE[0]:
        raise IndexError('latitude should be between -90 and 90 degrees')
    if ilat_0 == LTSHAPE[0]:
        ilat_0 = LTSHAPE[0] - 1  # need room to interpolate
    ilat_1 = ilat_0 + 1  # upper bound
    lon = lon % 360.0  # adjust longitude range from -180 - 180 to 0 - 360
    ilon_0 = int(lon / DY) % LTSHAPE[1]  # lower bound
    ilon_1 = (ilon_0 + 1) % LTSHAPE[1]  # upper bound
    f11 = LinkeConfig.linke_turbidity_table[ilat_0][ilon_0]
    f12 = LinkeConfig.linke_turbidity_table[ilat_0][ilon_1]
    f21 = LinkeConfig.linke_turbidity_table[ilat_1][ilon_0]
    f22 = LinkeConfig.linke_turbidity_table[ilat_1][ilon_1]
    f = np.asarray([[f11, f12], [f21, f22]])
    return wikipedia_interp2d(latitude, longitude, f, 90.0 - ilat_0 * DX, ilon_0 * DY)


TIME = pd.DatetimeIndex(['%d/1/2016' % m for m in xrange(1,13)])


def test():
    lookup_linke_turbidity_test = partial(
        lookup_linke_turbidity, filepath=LTFILE, interp_turbidity=False
    )
    test1 = [79, 179]
    lt_test1 = get_linke_turbidity_at_latlon(*test1)
    lt_bench1 = lookup_linke_turbidity_test(time, *test1)
    LOGGER.debug('test1:\n%r', lt_test1)
    LOGGER.debug('bench1:\n%r', lt_bench1)
    assert lt_test == lookup_linke_turbidity_test(time, *test1)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    print get_linke_turbidity_at_latlon(*[float(ll) for ll in sys.argv[1:]])

Let's try to follow common practices concerning deprecation and versioning.

I haven't had the time to document this but I think as DJA we should follow [Semver](https://semver.org/) and implement a similar deprecation policy like [Ember](https://www.emberjs.com/) (which is most likely the most used JS framework with JSON API spec).

This way we have a simple migration guide for our users. We can simply say, upgrade to the latest minor version of a series (e.g. 2.6.0) and solve all deprecation warnings. In version 3.0.0 we simply remove all deprecation warnings and do nothing else (real changes follow then in a version 3.1.0 etc.).

What this mean for our pagination:
* We keep the naming of `JSONAPIPageNumberPagination` till version 2.6.0
* In version 3.1.0 (or we could think of doing it in 3.0.0 as well) we make `JSONAPIPageNumberPagination` deprecated and change logic of `PageNumberPagination` to `JSONAPIPageNumberPagination` is today

This way we do not have to introduce a new arbitrary setting and have a simple migration guide for our DJA users.

What do you think?

> ```python
    forget_gate = lasagne.layers.Gate()     # init the gates outside of LSTM to use them for train and test
    input_gate = lasagne.layers.Gate()      # init the gates outside of LSTM to use them for train and test
    output_gate = lasagne.layers.Gate()     # init the gates outside of LSTM to use them for train and test
    cell = lasagne.layers.Gate(W_cell=None, nonlinearity=lasagne.nonlinearities.tanh)
```
This looks like it would do the correct thing, the trouble is that the `Gate()` only holds the *initializers* for the weights. If it wasn't for this indirection, you'd need to tell the `Gate()` about the correct shapes matching the LSTM layer. The actual instantiation of the parameters only happens in the `LSTMLayer` constructor: https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/blob/master/lasagne/layers/recurrent.py#L848-L858
At that point, `gate.W_in` is just `lasagne.init.Normal()`, because that's the default value in the `Gate()` constructor: http://lasagne.readthedocs.org/en/latest/modules/layers/recurrent.html#lasagne.layers.Gate

The easiest solution will be to instantiate the first `LSTMLayer` normally, then get out its parameters to construct fully-instantiated `Gate` objects to be passed to the second `LSTMLayer`. Basically, you'd invert this code: https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/blob/master/lasagne/layers/recurrent.py#L860-L872
For example:
```python
ingate = Gate(lstm1.W_in_to_ingate, lstm1.W_hid_to_ingate,
              lstm1.W_cell_to_ingate, lstm1.b_ingate, lstm1.nonlinearity_ingate)
```
Alternatively, you could instantiate all the `Gate()`s before (as you do now), passing manually-instantiated Theano shared variables of the correct shape to the constructor (instead of using the default arguments).

```python
l_LSTM_test.W_in_to_cell = l_LSTM_train.W_in_to_cell
```
This technique would also work, but you'd need to copy a lot more parameters. You could solve that using a loop over l_LSTM_train.__dict__, copying over all SharedVariable instances, but that'd be a bit of a hack.

> With regards to Keras's `compile` method, I suppose one reason to split this would be to make this step a bit more explicit, because it can take a long time.

nolearn.lasagne has `net.initialize()` that does the same thing as `compile` but it's implicitly called during `fit()` if it hasn't been called by the user before.

> I was also thinking about supporting more complicated use cases, i.e. what if there are multiple input layers, or what if there is no label.

nolearn.lasagne (and I think Keras too) allow you to pass a mapping of `{layer_name: input_value}` as `X`.  Multiple outputs aren't supported yet, but it's something on my list.  The use case for no labels is unsupervised training?  When I train auto-encoders, I will usually just pass the same thing (maybe transposed) as `X` and `y`.

> I also like `net.evaluate()` in Keras

nolearn.lasagne has `net.score(X, y)`, which is the same thing but the name's from scikit-learn  Again Keras uses a different method name for no reason apparently.

> I do think this would be a key addition to the library, but it could also become quite a mess trying to support every possible thing people may want (logging, web monitoring, callbacks for early stopping, checkpointing, etc.).

Callbacks (an idea which I believe Keras took from nolearn.lasagne) help a lot there.  There's a couple of well-defined hooks and people can add their own behaviour easily.  You can easily cover today all of the use cases that @ebenolson mentions with the available hooks: logging, web monitoring, callbacks for early stopping, checkpointing.  As an example, check out `EarlyStopping` in the kfkd tutorial, or consider how nolearn.lasagne.NeuralNet does its own [logging using a callback](https://github.com/dnouri/nolearn/blob/d4d3c3feb2ad6fa571e3a041f02875b7fcde352c/nolearn/lasagne/handlers.py#L15).

> I think we can easily sort this out by enabling people to make the training loop explicit as a `for` loop.

I know I know I can't stop, but nolearn.lasagne allows you to do this:

```python
net = NeuralNet(
    # ...
    max_epochs=1,  # a little hacky but you'll survive
    )

for X, y in mygenerator:
    net.fit(X, y)
    # fun stuff here
```

I still prefer using the hooks though because it's plug-and-play.  It's more declarative, and encourages reuse between code.  Here's an example of a `NeuralNet` that saves checkpoints, but only if the validation error has improved:

```python
net = NeuralNet(
    # bunch of stuff...
    on_epoch_finished=[
        SaveWeights(
            'models/model-{epoch}-{timestamp}.pkl',
            only_best=True,
            ),
        ],
    )
```

Hi, tried these:
```
python3 -m venv venv

```
Result: Pip installs latest available Django (2.0) and latest django-import-export. Expected. It will also install diff-match-patch, tablib, pytz, odfpy, openpyxl, unicodecsv, xlrd, xlwt and PyYAML.

It would be a nightmare to install every dependency with the correct version on that dependency tree. I want to use django-import-export, so I expect it to install anything needed.

```
python3 -m venv venv
. venv/bin/activate
pip install django==1.8.0
pip install -U django-import-export
```
Result: Pip installs django 1.8.0. Then pip install latest django-import-export, latest Django (2.0). Expected. It might be a surprise, but you asked for upgrade, consider this instead:

```
python3 -m venv venv
. venv/bin/activate
pip install django==1.8.0
pip install django-import-export
```
Result: you get django-import-export latest version and dependencies, Django version is unchanged.

```
python3 -m venv venv
. venv/bin/activate
pip install django==1.4.0
pip install -U django-import-export
```
Result: again, you ask for upgrade, you get django 2.0, if you try without upgrade option:

```
python3 -m venv venv
. venv/bin/activate
pip install django==1.4.0
pip install django-import-export
```
Result: you get Django 2.0, even when you didn't ask for it.

Seeing all the options, I'd say we keep current behaviour. If you want latest version of package X you should be aware it may require updating your old packages, including Django.

**About unicode.** The goal was to have the same API code for both Python 2 and 3 (e.g. the test code shouldn't be changed). It is possible to add Python 2 checks and returns str instead of unicode, however in that case we need to use CPython API for the strings manipulations and do not rely on Cython auto-generated code. Python 2 code requiring actual str type is not so common nowadays as a lot of developers writing cross-version code, so please provide me the example where it's actually happening, so I can add tests for this use-case.

**pcap_ex.c formatting.** I'd like to suggest reformatting it after the merge.

**pcap iterator.** It may be related to #49, I have to take a look. The code has been modified for the consistency, so the iterator works the same way as the loop() method and timeouts relate directly to the [libpcap](http://www.tcpdump.org/manpages/pcap.3pcap.html) function:

> _packet buffer timeout_
> If, when capturing, packets are delivered as soon as they arrive, the application capturing the packets will be woken up for each packet as it arrives, and might have to make one or more calls to the operating system to fetch each packet.
> If, instead, packets are not delivered as soon as they arrive, but are delivered after a short delay (called a "packet buffer timeout"), more than one packet can be accumulated before the packets are delivered, so that a single wakeup would be done for multiple packets, and each set of calls made to the operating system would supply multiple packets, rather than a single packet. This reduces the per-packet CPU overhead if packets are arriving at a high rate, increasing the number of packets per second that can be captured.
> The packet buffer timeout is required so that an application won't wait for the operating system's capture buffer to fill up before packets are delivered; if packets are arriving slowly, that wait could take an arbitrarily long period of time.

The last sentence is exactly the problem I've faced with npcap on Windows 10 - without timeout I had to wait until the capture buffer is filled and couldn't see the traffic in real time.

I guess what it boils down to from my point of view is that if you have a version number you have either one of two scenarios

1. you have a policy of which version numbers you will allow to be loaded (e.g. nothing older than a major version). This is artificial and the object might have actually loaded fine if the only schema change was to add new items or include allowed values. To make this number meaningful then you have to remember what is a safe compatibility when updating the version number as well as what minor/major version you can update it to for any given schema change.

2. you allow certain objects to be loaded based on some (your own or groups) knowledge of it being ok based on that number. This is not very stable as it relies on external knowledge and may mean knowing different safe version numbers for different objects.


Far better I'd say to try and load the object and if it cant load an object type or there is a missing schema attribute or the value is no longer valid then tell the user it's invalid.

After all the version numbers are only used as a guide as to whether to load or not. If we decide based on version number that the object are incompatible then this would have been the outcome of trying to load it anyway. If we decide to load it based on version number then presumably it would have loaded anyway as it was just an additional schema attribute which would be defaulted or an extra allowed value. This is what I meant by essentially a boolean as you still have to attempt the loading after you have made the version based decision.

I've tried really hard to reproduce this and failing. 

I *think* the current solution isn't thread-safe but it's hard to prove because of the way transactions are done in kinto. It's not immediately clear where the commit happens after the `kinto_client.create()` record happens. 

The SQL query can do three things:

1. Do nothing, just return the row unchanged (inserted=FALSE)
2. Insert the record (inserted=TRUE)
3. Update the existing record and change `deleted=TRUE` (inserted=TRUE)

If the first thing happens, it will raise a `UnicityError`. (I guess that's to inform the client that you tried to create a record that already existed!)

But it's not clear where the `COMMIT` happens. I suspect what might happens is that record is changing during an insert. But it's not clear either. 

One interesting thing to remember is that we've seen a lot of these errors in buildhub. In buildhub, we never delete things. I.e. we never set `deleted=TRUE` on any records. So what happens there is probably concurrent calls to `kinto_client.create(same, arguments, in, concurrent, threads)`. But the strange thing is that if you try to call this SQL with the same it should raise an exception like:
```
duplicate key value violates unique constraint "idx_records_parent_id_collection_id_last_modified"
DETAIL:  Key (parent_id, collection_id, last_modified)=(/buckets/build-hub/collections/releases, record, 2018-04-16 09:39:00.004) already exists.
```

It concerns me what this can happen. Perhaps we can work together to audit how the transactions are committed. I really think there are better patterns to insert something

> There are a few other locations that seem to use lists to index things (and to return indicies), line 682, and the lines surrounding 709 come to immediate attention.

Right.  I will take a closer look and fix those ones in a separate PR after more testing with numpy 1.15.

`maxlevel` is the argument used to set the number of levels.  It may not be the most intuitive, but if you access a particular coefficient, the `level` attribute tells you what level of the decomposition it corresponds to.  For example for the code
```python
import pywt
import numpy as np
img = np.random.random((200, 200))
wp = pywt.WaveletPacket2D(img, wavelet='db1', maxlevel=2)
```

I have specified a maximum of 2 levels.  I can access via keys corresponding to the characters 'a', 'v', 'h', 'd', corresponding the approximation and various detail coefficients.  Repeated characters are used to access higher levels of decomposition (e.g. ['aa'] for the second level approximation coefficients)

`wp['h'].level` will be `1` (wp['h'] are the first level horizontal detail coefficients)
`wp['hv'].level` would be `2`.  
`wp['hhh'].level` would return an `IndexError` because the transform was called with `maxlevel=2`.

You can also see some examples of usage and plotting the various coefficients in this demo:
https://github.com/PyWavelets/pywt/blob/5d23d72e984025b792a25ab7284581b3afb0e4f0/demo/wp_2d.py

class ApiDocsTestCase(unittest.TestCase, utils.SmokeTest):
    """Test whether API auto generated docs are available.

    This test targets the following issues:

    * `Pulp #3552 <https://pulp.plan.io/issues/3552>`_
    * `Pulp Smash #893 <https://github.com/PulpQE/pulp-smash/issues/893>`_
    """

    def setUp(self):
        """Create an API Client."""
        cfg = config.get_config()
        if selectors.bug_is_untestable(3552, cfg.pulp_version):
            self.skipTest('https://pulp.plan.io/issues/3552')
        self.client = api.Client(cfg)

    def test_valid_credentials(self):
        """Get API documentation with valid credentials.

        Assert the API documentation is returned.
        """
        self.client.get(API_DOCS_PATH)

    def test_no_credentials(self):
        """Get API documentation with no credentials.

        Assert the API documentation is returned.
        """
        del self.client.request_kwargs['auth']
        self.client.get(API_DOCS_PATH)

    def test_http_method(self):
        """Get API documentation with an HTTP method other than GET.

        Assert an error is returned.
        """
        with self.assertRaises(HTTPError):
            self.client.post(API_DOCS_PATH)

Hey @benjaminjkraft,

Even if a resolver is defined as a `classmethod`, it needs to receive a `root` (parent) element.
So the classmethod resolvers would look something like:

```python
class MyObjectType(graphene.ObjectType):
    name = graphene.String()
    @classname
    def resolve_name(cls, root, args, context, info):
        return 'My Name'
```

I know it sounds a little bit counter intuitive but all resolvers needs to receive the parent element they are working with.

Hi Giedrius,

The Joiner Device Credential and PSKd are essentially the same thing, the
only difference is the encoding.

The PSKd is in raw binary form, and is what the underlying OpenThread code
uses.

The specification section on base32-thread encoding of the Joiner Device
Credential is a presentation layer advisory.  The PSKd needs to be
presented to the user in a standard way across the entire user experience:

   - on the device label
   - when typed into a phone
   - within the QR code

If one vendor renders the PSKd in ASCII HEX, another in base-64, and a
third in base32-thread, there would be interoperability problems.  For
example, the standard Commissioning app wouldn't know how to convert what
the user types in into binary without extra information.

Martin

Martin Turon  |  Nest Labs

Same problem, I'll add a use case to move the discussion along:

I want to use FT to classify questions from users in a chatbot app. Input is like "I want to sign up", "How do I get a login", "How do I get started?". The chatbot will eventually be able to classify many types of user input, but until I've collected actual questions from users, I want to seed the "signup" class of questions with a small number (<100) of inputs that I make up, so that my app knows "this is a signup request". 

Problem:
With default settings for FT trainer on very few (but closely related - all with word 'signup', for example) observations, the predictions are not helpful... if I have 3 classes on 100 examples, I get probabilities of ~ 33% no matter what the input is, including gibberish input ("abc123").

Perhaps FT is inherently a bad choice for tiny datasets and early stage deployment like this. It's such a great tool for larger datasets that I was hoping to get it integrated into the app early on.

Release/move would leave behind `Py_None`. The codegen for moves balances out: `Py_None` would require and additional `++refcnt` in the move constructor/assignment, but the destructor of the move-out object would use the simpler `Py_DECREF` without the extra conditional of the `X` variant required for `nullptr`. While that just kind of balances out, regular copies and construction / destruction will definitely be leaner with the never-`nullptr` `object`, since it will always be able to use the regular `Py_INCREF/Py_DECREF` instead of their `X` variants. That would definitely benefit codegen and this `X` and non-`X` refcounting would be the main implementation difference between `object` and `owned_handle`.

Other than code size, there are two other benefits to having distinct types:

 * `object` can have a debug-only check for `nullptr` in its constructor.
 * Distinct types send a clear signal: always check for `nullptr` with one, never with the other. 

If there is only one type, these expectations need to be maintained some other way: either specifically documented or implied by the detail/interface separation. But that is an additional thing to think about or forget. `owned_handle` would be an implementation detail and a nice danger indicator.

@TheNavigat, I don't think that would work. We never make commits directly on `master`. In fact, we've disabled pushing to `master` on GitHub through [protected branches](https://help.github.com/articles/about-protected-branches/). All changes must go through a PR that has passed Travis tests.

Our general release process is as follows:
1. Push a [tag](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/tags) containing the version bump and the new `certbot-auto` and `letsencrypt-auto`s.
2. Upload Certbot packages to PyPI.
3. Make a PR to merge those changes into `master`.

With the system you're proposing, I believe it would be impossible to ever change `*-auto` unless we removed our protections on `master` which we're not going to do. If someone makes a PR trying to change `*-auto` without having already created a tag and released PyPI packages, I think it is correct behavior that the tests fail. The released versions of the scripts have not changed, so changing them in `master` should not be allowed.

For me it worked with Python 3.5.2 (also tried Python 2.7, but that did not work). I tried the 32-bit binary of Python, i.e. did not test 64-bit, but it may work as well.

1. Install Python 3.5.2 32bit.
2. Install setuptools using pip: `pip install setuptools` (use an **elevated** prompt).
3. Install Visual Studio Community 2015.
4. Download the SCIP library for Windows/PC, 32bit, msvc 14.0 from http://scip.zib.de/#download. You will need the files _libscip-3.2.1.mingw.x86.msvc.opt.spx.lib_ and _libscip-3.2.1.mingw.x86.msvc.opt.spx.dll_ from the zip archive.
5. In your PySCIPOpt directory, copy _libscip-3.2.1.mingw.x86.msvc.opt.spx.lib_ to the _lib_ subdirectory.
6. Copy the attached
[mysetup.txt](https://github.com/SCIP-Interfaces/PySCIPOpt/files/594935/mysetup.txt) to your PySCIPOpt directory and rename it to _mysetup.py_. (It is basically the original setup file with distutils replaced by setuptools, the environment variable stuff commented out and runtime_library_dirs removed.)
7. From your Windows kickoff menu, open Visual Studio 2015 x86 Native Tools Commandline. (This will
set up some environment variables correctly for the compilation of the cython module. If you later get an error like _"unable to find vcvarsall.bat"_, you failed on this one.) Go to your PySCIPOpt folder `cd C:\path\to\PySCIPOpt` and run `python mysetup.py install --user`. (If you used an elevated command prompt, you could skip the `--user` and install PySCIPOpt for all users.)
8. Copy _libscip-3.2.1.mingw.x86.msvc.opt.spx.dll_ to a folder in your PATH variable or to the directory where your python script is located and try using your application.

Good luck!

While working on #244, I uncovered something that might be useful, assume these models:

```python
class Parent(PolymorphicModel):
    pass

class ChildA(Parent):
    name = models.CharField()

class ChildB(Parent):
    height = models.IntegerField()
```

This works:
```python
obj = Parent.objects.non_polymorphic().select_related('childa__name')
obj.childa.name  # return the name!
```

Although this doesn't:
```python
Parent.objects.select_related('childa__name')
# childa is an invalid field name here
```

So we clearly don't need to fiddle with django's internals to get this issue fixed. I believe that we can actually deal with the `select_related` when casting the polymorphic instances to their real child class. It's just that the API `select_related(*fields)` doesn't _feel_ right. Maybe polymorphic querysets need something different, like a mapping of `child_class: related_fields`.

New behaviour:

* During testenv_create stage, if `.tox/{venv}` does not exist it is created
* During testenv_create, or tox_testenv_install_deps or tox_runtest stages, if `.tox/{venv}/Pipfile` does not exist, the `./Pipfile` from the root of the project will be cloned to the `.tox/{venv}/Pipfile` path.
* Whilst executing `pipenv` via the `graph`, `install` or `run` commands for envreport, install_deps and test stages respectively, `PIPENV_PIPFILE` is set to `.tox/{venv}/Pipfile`

> This flag would obviously conflict with [travis:env] use, and would need to raise a configuration exc.

I'm not sure what you mean. The flag would hide basically all of the current functionality that gets configured in the `[travis]` _and_ the `[travis:env]` sections, to make it opt-in. Nothing would change or break, apart from adding a flag (`--detect` or `--auto-envlist`).

> A disable flag seems acceptable, since this sounds like a minority case.

It certainly would be a minority case currently. If you're not using this feature in the current package, there's really nothing less. That's why I brought up the future feature of reporting jobs to GitHub. That's one case that I can readily see a desire to have Tox work as it does normally, and just use a particular feature that we're adding to it. A positive flag would keep feature usage consistent.

If the negative flag is preferred, and likely even if it is not, I think a `--no-detect` or `--no-auto-envlist` is the appropriate spelling.

hi @robertnishihara, sorry that I missed your comment.

First, yes, it's a straightforward change, if each worker still has its own object store and raylet client (see my RP #2244). 
But in order to make it more scalable, I think it makes sense to have multiple workers share one connection in the future. However, that would require larger code changes, because right now the a lot of code in node manager looks up the worker by its connection, rather than by an unique id. 
So, let's see how #2244 works and decide wether to make the change afterwards. 

Regarding the API change to support coroutines in Python, what needs to be done is to make all the APIs async compatible (which means make all the underlying IO async and `async def` all functions) (I found there's already an issue discussing this topic #493).

Then the worker will be something like this:
```python
class Worker:
    async def loop(self):
        while True:
            task = await self.get_next_task()
            await self.execute_task(task);
```
And that requires users to define their tasks as async functions as well.

I didn't dig deeply into supporting coroutine in Java. After all, multi-threading is much more common in Java than coroutines. And I think it's okay that each language has its own concurrency model. The core doesn't need to know about this.
I haven't tested it personally but it may be worth evaluating at some point

Chatted with @ericl offline: The existing semantics of `num_returns` is to return **at most** the number of object ids specified by `num_returns`. This implementation changes the semantics to be return **at least** the number of object ids specified by `num_returns`. For instance, if you do `ray.wait` on 100 objects with `num_returns=2`, and 50 objects are done, then `ray.wait` returns 50 objects instead of just 2.

Here are some options:
1. Change the semantics of `num_returns`, which solves #1128. @ericl notes this breaks syntax such as `[x,y] = ray.wait(object_ids, num_returns=2)`.
2. Keep existing semantics, but come up with a way to be "fair" with which objects are returned. Some ideas include random sampling or stratified sampling over nodes. We should include a test that captures these semantics.

@robertnishihara can you provide your thoughts on this?

Here's a working implementation:

```python
from functools import wraps
def pause(send, receive):
    def f(iterator):
        @wraps(iterator)
        def inner():
            i = iterator()
            def next_i(change):
                try:
                    send[0].set_trait(send[1], i.send(change.new))
                except StopIteration as e:
                    send[0].set_trait(send[1], e.value)
                    receive[0].unobserve(next_i, receive[1])
            receive[0].observe(next_i, receive[1])
            send[0].set_trait(send[1], next(i))
        return inner
    return f
```

Meanwhile back in the subthread about possibly making `await ...(...)` a single piece of syntax:

> OK, I think I see your argument now, and given the leading `await` you should be able to make it work even within the constraints of the LL(1) parsing restriction

I was nervous about this b/c I don't know a lot about parsing, but actually it looks trivial: the [grammar rule](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/grammar.html) that currently handles `await` expressions is `atom_expr: [AWAIT] atom trailer*`, where `AWAIT` is the `await` token and `trailer*` is the function call parentheses (among other things). So in fact `await ...(...)` already is a single piece of syntax as far as the parser is concerned; it's only split into `await ...` and `...(...)` during the concrete syntax → AST transformation.

Honestly, I'm not even sure I'm going to keep that interrupt thing.   At one point, I did have this priority mechanism, but after some further thought, didn't like the idea of putting that much implicit magic into the kernel.  Instead, I pushed it out to "user space".  A task will get an exception if there's a conflict. It can interrupt the other waiting task if it wants.  Beyond that, the handling is "someone else's problem."  I suppose a higher level Streams API could incorporate something more advanced around this, but from the kernel point of view, I wanted to keep it simple.

> if we call hasattr for every supported attribute, I think we might as well just do it once during `__init__`

Does freezing/caching the attribute lists like this simplify the code, though? `__dir__` is extremely *not* performance sensitive, and usually more state = bad.

> Also, is `Closing(Async)ContextManager` undesirable (making `async with await` the only supported context manager convention)?

Yeah, this is what I was trying to say somewhere up above... the `await` should be mandatory.

Rationale: I don't think `async with await open_file(...)` is the prettiest, but I like it better than having the same function support being called like `await open_file(...)` and like `async with open_file(...)` at the same time. Trio functions should pick one of those and stick to it; anything else is too magic and breaks the simplifying illusion that the difference between async functions and regular functions is just that async functions require `await` when called (see e.g. the tutorial). And we kind of have to support `await open_file(...)`. So `async with open_file(...)` loses. "Special cases aren't special enough to break the rules", as `import this` says. Plus since we have to support both `await open_file(...)` and `async with file_obj`, the `async with await open_file(...)` case falls out as supported automatically, whereas the `async with open_file(...)` case would be an extra thing people would have to memorize ("there should by one – and preferably only one – obvious way to do it").

If it makes you feel better, people will also be writing things like `async with await open_tcp_stream(hostname, port)` as soon as I finish implementing the high level stream code :-)

Changed the aerosol indirect treatment again! This still uses Ghan's model as its basis but now uses a functional relationship rather than a radial basis function. This is calculated faster and is more reliable and transparent. I borrowed the treatment of the aerosol indirect effect from Stevens (2015) and extended it to primary organic aerosol (i.e., BC+OC) as well as sulfate (as in Ghan's model the indirect effect depends on both).

Leighton and I are aiming to develop something more sophisticated in the future based on the work he has done with emulators in Carslaw et al (2013). But for now this does a nice job. This plot shows how ERFaci changes with emissions of POA and SOx following the output from Ghan's model (points) and my curve fit (surface).

![supp1](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7061148/36946296-ce3326b2-1fb2-11e8-8342-8b3e7433aece.png)

For the model I then scale this output from this plot above to match the best estimate of present day ERFaci from AR5. Users can turn off this scaling if they desire.

```
from django import template

2. And then just call it in template : 

```
{{ post.content|show_markdown|safe }}

The problem is the long sample name, matplotlib gets overwelmed and tries to pack everything in the figure axes, generating this messy result.

Let's try to solve this for your use case.
If you look at the source code ( [plot_fractions documentation](http://velocyto.org/velocyto.py/_modules/velocyto/analysis.html#VelocytoLoom.plot_fractions)) and get definition of plot fraction method. You can redefine it as a function in your workspace and edit the matplotlib calls, for example to hide the legend box. I did this below:

```
    def plot_fractions(self, save2file=None):
        """Plots a barplot showing the abundance of spliced/unspliced molecules in the dataset

        Arguments
        ---------
        save2file: str (default: None)
            If not None specifies the file path to which plots get saved

        Returns
        -------
        Nothing, it plots a barplot
        """
        plt.figure(figsize=(3.2, 5))
        try:
            chips, chip_ix = np.unique(self.ca["SampleID"], return_inverse=1)
        except KeyError:
            chips, chip_ix = np.unique([i.split(":")[0] for i in self.ca["CellID"]], return_inverse=1)
        n = len(chips)
        for i in np.unique(chip_ix):
            tot_mol_cell_submatrixes = [X[:, chip_ix == i].sum(0) for X in [self.S, self.A, self.U]]
            total = np.sum(tot_mol_cell_submatrixes, 0)
            _mean = [np.mean(j / total) for j in tot_mol_cell_submatrixes]
            _std = [np.std(j / total) for j in tot_mol_cell_submatrixes]
            plt.ylabel("Fraction")
            plt.bar(np.linspace(-0.2, 0.2, n)[i] + np.arange(3), _mean, 0.5 / (n * 1.05), label=chips[i])
            plt.errorbar(np.linspace(-0.2, 0.2, n)[i] + np.arange(3), _mean, _std, c="k", fmt="none", lw=1, capsize=2)

            # Hide the right and top spines
            plt.gca().spines['right'].set_visible(False)
            plt.gca().spines['top'].set_visible(False)
            # Only show ticks on the left and bottom spines
            plt.gca().yaxis.set_ticks_position('left')
            plt.gca().xaxis.set_ticks_position('bottom')
            plt.gca().spines['left'].set_bounds(0, 0.8)
            # plt.legend() # REMOVED
            
        plt.xticks(np.arange(3), ["spliced", "ambiguous", "unspliced"])
        plt.tight_layout()
        if save2file:
            plt.savefig(save2file, bbox_inches="tight")
```

Finally call it on your vlm object

```
plot_fractions(vlm)
```

Notable "breaking" changes that I would like comments on:
- `canonical_variables` is now a set instead of an OrderedSet
- `aliases()` returns a set instead of an OrderedSet
- `__iter__`   no longer loops in a guaranteed order (because of the OrderedSet -> set change)

> Hmmm... given the time pyzmq is in use and the number of users I've seen (irc mostly) I'd say:
> You'll have a bad time _changing_ the API.
> A new (better) way using new methods will be the only way I guess.
> It's the same as with the core libs. In general: If an API is in use, forget changing it.

Yes, I have no intention of removing the existing (bad) API in the foreseeable future for this reason.  But I don't want the new API to appear as if it's sugar wrapped around the old API.  Docs, examples, etc. should *never mention* the old API, and it should never occur to new users to use it.  That's why I don't like the idea of writing docs with `Stream.on_recv_stream` all over the place, because it would mean that the primary API is a redundant name, referring to itself as a special case of something never mentioned elsewhere.

An alternative would be to continue to use the same on_recv name, but switch to the new behavior with a `from zmq.__future__ import on_recv_stream` style import.
